mainText:
If you only remember one thing today, remember this: China's Moonshot A I has stopped competing on cheap. Kimi K three is priced and benchmarked like a U S frontier model, and it goes open weight this week.

Number one. Kimi K three, the least Chinese, Chinese model. From your Recode China A I newsletter: Moonshot launched K three last Thursday, calling it an ambitious, even risky attempt to shed the old stereotype of Chinese models as a cheap alternative. It is a two point eight trillion parameter mixture of experts model with a million token context window. Moonshot claims it beats Claude Opus four point eight and G P T five point five on coding and agent benchmarks, trailing only Fable five and G P T five point six. Pricing is three dollars in, fifteen dollars out per million tokens, the most expensive Chinese model ever. Open weights are promised by July twenty seventh. The launch rattled tech markets. Why it matters: if a Chinese open weight model performs at near frontier level, U S pricing power and Washington's containment strategy both get harder.

Number two. Anthropic closes the book on Fable access. From your Rundown newsletter: after the June export control shutdown of Fable five and Mythos five, which was lifted June thirtieth, and two access extensions in July, the extension window is now ending. The nineteen day shutdown is being read as a lesson in A I as infrastructure risk. Why it matters: frontier model access is now a geopolitical variable, not just a product decision. Plan for it like a cloud outage.

Number three. The July model wave. Your A I News from swyx called yesterday a quiet day. Fair, after a month that delivered G P T five point six with a million token context, Claude Sonnet five at introductory pricing, and Meta's first paid developer A P I. Meanwhile your A I Collective newsletter asks why Google threw out a nearly done A I model and started over, with a thirty one billion dollar Chinese rival waiting. Why it matters: the frontier race is now four way, and pricing is the new battlefield.

Number four. A I policy round up. The F T C wants public comment by July thirty first on state laws that require altering truthful A I outputs. Illinois signed its A I Safety Measures Act, with catastrophic risk frameworks and seventy two hour incident reporting. States have now enacted over one hundred A I laws. China's rules for A I agents became enforceable last week. And the E U folded cybersecurity and A I into a single action plan. Why it matters: compliance is fragmenting fast. The voluntary ethics era is formally over.

Number five. Chips and geopolitics. From your Recode China A I weekly: Xi made his first appearance at the World A I Conference, and an eight and a half billion dollar Chinese memory chip I P O is moving. On the U S side, Nvidia H two hundred sales to China now get case by case review. Analysts describe a fully bifurcated chip ecosystem, with China's new five year plan codifying self reliance. Also, Alphabet is reportedly building a more efficient A I chip. Why it matters: two parallel, incompatible A I hardware stacks are forming. Every supply chain bet now has to pick a side.

Number six. Surveillance outlasts the World Cup. From your Tech Policy Press newsletter: the World Cup ends, the surveillance continues. The tournament's biometric and monitoring infrastructure across host cities will not be dismantled with the stadiums. Why it matters for your comms work: event justified surveillance that becomes permanent is a story arc worth tracking, and the messaging around it will be contested.

Number seven. Business quick hits. Databricks hits a one hundred eighty eight billion dollar valuation. A M D lands Microsoft for Helios, its first rack scale A I system to rival Nvidia. And Samsung moves its U S headquarters teams to Texas, chasing the A I infrastructure buildout. Why it matters: enterprise A I money is consolidating around a few very large bets.

Number eight, your good news. Jeff Bezos is backing Cusp A I, which is hunting new chip materials with Nvidia. That is A I driven materials discovery aimed at more efficient hardware. And M I T Technology Review's new issue focuses on interpretability, understanding why models answer the way they do, and on A I agents as scientific collaborators. That's the top of the news. Your full briefing is in the recording.
